chapters.title: What is Django, and why a "framework" at all?
chapters.navTitle: What is Django, and why a "framework" at all?
chapters.lead: Django is a full-featured Python web frameworkA pre-built structure and toolset for building applications, handling common problems for you.press Enter or click to read more
chapters.html:
Groundwork · Chapter 00

What is Django, and why a "framework" at all?

Django is a full-featured Python web frameworkA pre-built structure and toolset for building applications, handling common problems for you.
press Enter or click to read more: batteries included, opinionated, and built to get a complete, working web application running fast.

Without a framework, building TastyGo's backend from raw Python would mean writing your own URL-matching logic, your own database connection handling, your own HTML templating, your own admin interface, your own security protections, all before writing a single line of actual TastyGo-specific logic. Django provides all of that already, solved and battle-tested, so you focus on what makes TastyGo TastyGo.

The plain-language version
Building a restaurant from raw materials means sourcing your own wood, forging your own nails, building your own tables. A framework is closer to leasing a fully fitted-out restaurant space: kitchen, tables, and utilities already there, so you focus on the food and the menu, exactly what makes your restaurant yours.
Common trip-ups
  • Assuming a framework does everything for you. Django removes a huge amount of repetitive infrastructure work, but your application's actual logic and design are still entirely up to you.
  • Fighting the framework's conventions. Django is "opinionated," it has a preferred way of doing things; working with that grain is far smoother than working against it.
0.1

Recap

Django is a batteries-included Python web framework, providing URL routing, database access, templating, an admin interface, and security defaults out of the box, so you build application-specific logic instead of infrastructure.

Easy
What does a framework like Django primarily provide?

chapters.title: Django's toolkit: manage.py, apps, and project structure
chapters.navTitle: Django's toolkit: manage.py, apps, and project structure
chapters.lead: A new Django project has a predictable shape, worth knowing before diving into any one piece of it.
chapters.html:
Groundwork · Chapter 0b

Django's toolkit: manage.py, apps, and project structure

A new Django project has a predictable shape, worth knowing before diving into any one piece of it.

tastygo/
    manage.py            # command-line tool for running/managing the project
    tastygo/
        settings.py       # project-wide configuration
        urls.py           # top-level URL routing
    orders/               # one Django "app": a self-contained feature area
        models.py         # this app's data
        views.py          # this app's request-handling logic
        urls.py           # this app's own URL patterns

manage.py is the command-line entry point for nearly everything: running the dev server, creating migrations, creating an admin user. A Django "project" is the whole site; each "app" inside it is a self-contained feature area (orders, restaurants, accounts), which keeps a growing codebase organized rather than one giant tangle of files.

Common trip-ups
  • Confusing a Django "project" with a Django "app." A project is the whole site; an app is one feature area within it. One project can, and usually does, contain several apps.
  • Cramming everything into one giant app. Splitting by feature area (orders, restaurants, accounts) keeps each app focused and easier to reason about as the project grows.
0.1

Recap

manage.py is the command-line entry point. A project is the whole site; apps are self-contained feature areas within it, each with their own models, views, and URLs.

Easy
What is the difference between a Django "project" and a Django "app"?

chapters.title: Meet the project: building TastyGo's backend in Django
chapters.navTitle: Meet the project: building TastyGo's backend in Django
chapters.lead: Same TastyGo used across every CareerLadder course. This time, you're designing the actual Django project that would power it.
chapters.html:
Groundwork · Chapter 0i

Meet the project: building TastyGo's backend in Django

Same TastyGo used across every CareerLadder course. This time, you're designing the actual Django project that would power it.

TastyGo's Django project will have three apps mirroring exactly the tables you already know from SQLingo: customers, restaurants, and orders. Each chapter ahead builds one real piece of this: models mapping to those tables, views handling requests about them, templates displaying them, and eventually an admin panel for managing them, all without writing raw SQL by hand, the ORM (chapter 09 onward) generates it for you.

0.1

Recap

TastyGo's Django backend mirrors the same customers/restaurants/orders structure from SQLingo, giving you a concrete, familiar target to build toward chapter by chapter.

Easy
TastyGo's Django project will have apps for which three areas?

chapters.title: Projects vs. apps
chapters.lead: Deciding how to split a Django project into apps is one of the first real design decisions you'll make.
chapters.html:
Part I · Chapter 01

Projects vs. apps

Deciding how to split a Django project into apps is one of the first real design decisions you'll make.

A reasonable split for TastyGo
accountscustomer sign-up, login, profile
restaurantsrestaurant listings, menus, ratings
ordersplacing, tracking, and refunding orders

Each app can (and should) be reasonably self-contained: orders needing to reference a restaurant or a customer is normal and expected, but an app that reaches deep into unrelated logic across the whole project is usually a sign the split needs rethinking.

Common trip-ups
  • Splitting apps too finely. An app per model is usually overkill; group closely related functionality together.
  • One giant "core" app holding everything. This defeats the entire purpose of splitting into apps in the first place.
1.1

Recap

Split a project into apps by feature area, each reasonably self-contained, neither too fine-grained nor one giant catch-all.

Medium
TastyGo wants to add restaurant-owner-facing tools (managing their own menu, seeing their own orders). Would you add this to the existing "restaurants" app, or create a new app? Explain your reasoning.

chapters.title: settings.py and configuration
chapters.lead: Every project-wide decision, which database to use, which apps are installed, security settings, lives in one file.
chapters.html:
Part I · Chapter 02

settings.py and configuration

Every project-wide decision, which database to use, which apps are installed, security settings, lives in one file.

INSTALLED_APPS = [
    "django.contrib.admin",
    "django.contrib.auth",
    "orders",
    "restaurants",
]

DATABASES = {
    "default": {
        "ENGINE": "django.db.backends.postgresql",
        "NAME": "tastygo",
    }
}

DEBUG = True   # never True in production, chapter 28

INSTALLED_APPS tells Django which apps (yours and built-in ones like the admin) are actually active. DATABASES configures which real database engine to connect to, exactly what the Fundamentals course's "what a database actually is" chapter was preparing you for, this is where you'd plug in PostgreSQL, MySQL, or SQLite.

Common trip-ups
  • Forgetting to add a new app to INSTALLED_APPS. An app that exists as a folder but isn't registered here won't have its models recognized by migrations, or its admin registered.
  • Leaving DEBUG = True in production. This can leak sensitive internal information (like full stack traces) to real users; covered in depth in chapter 28.
2.1

Recap

settings.py centralizes project-wide configuration: installed apps, database connection, and security-relevant flags like DEBUG.

Easy
What happens if you create a new Django app but forget to add it to INSTALLED_APPS?

chapters.title: The MTV pattern
chapters.lead: Django organizes every request around three roles: Model, Template, View, each with one clear job.
chapters.html:
Part I · Chapter 03

The MTV pattern

Django organizes every request around three roles: Model, Template, View, each with one clear job.

The URL router (urls.py) picks a view based on the request path. The view asks the Model layer for whatever data it needs, then hands that data to a Template, which renders it into real HTML sent back as the response. Each piece only does its own job: models don't format HTML, templates don't query the database directly.

Common trip-ups
  • Putting database queries directly inside a template. Templates are for presentation only; data-fetching belongs in the view.
  • Confusing Django's MTV with the more commonly known MVC pattern. They're closely related; Django's "View" plays the role most other frameworks call a "Controller," and Django's "Template" plays the role most call a "View."
3.1

Recap

MTV splits responsibility cleanly: Models hold and query data, Views contain request-handling logic, Templates render the final HTML, connected by URL routing that decides which view handles which request.

Easy
In Django's MTV pattern, which piece is responsible for actually querying the database?

chapters.title: The dev server, conceptually
chapters.lead: python manage.py runserver
chapters.html:
Part I · Chapter 04

The dev server, conceptually

python manage.py runserver starts a lightweight web server for local development, connecting everything from this course into something you can actually visit in a browser.

$ python manage.py runserver
Watching for file changes with StatReloader
Starting development server at http://127.0.0.1:8000/

This dev server is deliberately simple and not meant for real users, exactly the "dev environment" concept from the Fundamentals course: fast to restart, forgiving, and never what you'd actually deploy. The DevOps course later covers what does run Django in production (a real WSGI/ASGI server, often behind a reverse proxy like nginx).

Common trip-ups
  • Using runserver in production. It's explicitly documented as unsuitable for real traffic; it lacks the performance and security hardening a real deployment needs.
4.1

Recap

runserver is a simple, convenient local development server, never meant for production traffic, which uses a proper WSGI/ASGI server instead.

Easy
Why shouldn't Django's runserver be used to serve real production traffic?

chapters.title: urls.py and URL patterns
chapters.lead: URL routingThe system that matches an incoming request's URL to the view that should handle it.press Enter or click to read more
chapters.html:
Part II · Chapter 05

urls.py and URL patterns

URL routingThe system that matches an incoming request's URL to the view that should handle it.
press Enter or click to read more is a list of patterns, checked top to bottom, each mapped to the view that should handle a matching request.

from django.urls import path
from . import views

urlpatterns = [
    path("restaurants/", views.restaurant_list),
    path("restaurants/nearby/", views.nearby_restaurants),
]

Django checks each pattern in order and uses the first one that matches the request's path. A request for /restaurants/nearby/ matches the second pattern; note that if it were listed before a broader pattern that could also match it, order would matter a great deal.

Common trip-ups
  • Ordering a broad, catch-all pattern before a more specific one. Since Django stops at the first match, a broad pattern listed first can silently "steal" requests meant for a more specific pattern listed after it.
5.1

Recap

urlpatterns is an ordered list of path-to-view mappings, checked top to bottom; the first matching pattern wins.

Easy
If a broad pattern like path("restaurants//") is listed before path("restaurants/nearby/"), what happens to a request for /restaurants/nearby/?

chapters.title: Path converters and dynamic URLs
chapters.lead: Most real URLs aren't fixed text, they contain a variable part, like which specific order you're looking at.
chapters.html:
Part II · Chapter 06

Path converters and dynamic URLs

Most real URLs aren't fixed text, they contain a variable part, like which specific order you're looking at.

urlpatterns = [
    path("orders//", views.order_detail),
    path("restaurants//", views.restaurant_detail),
]

def order_detail(request, order_id):
    # order_id arrives already converted to an int
    ...
Common converters
<int:x>matches digits, converts to a Python int
<str:x>matches any non-slash text
<slug:x>matches URL-friendly text (letters, numbers, hyphens, underscores)

The converted value is passed directly into the view function as a parameter with that same name, already the right Python type, so order_id arrives as a real int, not a string you'd need to convert yourself.

Common trip-ups
  • Using <str:x> when <int:x> is more correct. <int:x> also validates the URL actually contains digits, rejecting non-numeric input before it ever reaches your view.
6.1

Recap

Path converters (<int:x>, <str:x>, <slug:x>) capture a variable part of the URL, validate its shape, and pass it into the view already converted to the right Python type.

Medium
Write a urls.py pattern for viewing a specific restaurant by its numeric id, at a path like /restaurants/42/, mapped to a view called restaurant_detail.

chapters.title: Named URLs and reverse()
chapters.lead: Hard-coding a URL path as a string everywhere it's used is fragile. Naming a URL pattern lets you refer to it by name instead.
chapters.html:
Part II · Chapter 07

Named URLs and reverse()

Hard-coding a URL path as a string everywhere it's used is fragile. Naming a URL pattern lets you refer to it by name instead.

path("orders//", views.order_detail, name="order-detail")

# elsewhere, instead of hard-coding "/orders/42/":
from django.urls import reverse
url = reverse("order-detail", args=[42])

If the actual URL structure ever changes (say, /orders/<id>/ becomes /order/<id>/details/), every place using reverse("order-detail", ...) keeps working automatically, since it looks the path up by name rather than assuming it stays fixed text forever.

Common trip-ups
  • Hard-coding URL strings throughout templates and views. This breaks silently the moment a URL pattern changes; named URLs with reverse() (or the equivalent {% url %} template tag) avoid this entirely.
7.1

Recap

Naming a URL pattern and using reverse() (or {% url %} in templates) to generate links to it means URL structure can change in one place without breaking every reference to it.

Medium
Why prefer reverse("order-detail", args=[42]) over hard-coding the string "/orders/42/" throughout the codebase?

chapters.title: Including an app's URLs
chapters.lead: Each app typically owns its own urls.py, and the project's top-level urls.py simply includes them, keeping URL routing organized the same way apps organize everything else.
chapters.html:
Part II · Chapter 08

Including an app's URLs

Each app typically owns its own urls.py, and the project's top-level urls.py simply includes them, keeping URL routing organized the same way apps organize everything else.

# tastygo/urls.py (project-level)
from django.urls import path, include

urlpatterns = [
    path("orders/", include("orders.urls")),
    path("restaurants/", include("restaurants.urls")),
]

# orders/urls.py (app-level)
urlpatterns = [
    path("/", views.order_detail),
]

A request for /orders/42/ first matches orders/ at the project level, then Django continues matching the rest of the path (42/) against orders/urls.py. This keeps each app's URL patterns self-contained and independent of the others.

Common trip-ups
  • Defining every URL pattern directly in the project-level urls.py. This quickly becomes unmanageable as the project grows; include() keeps each app's routing scoped to itself.
8.1

Recap

include() lets the project-level urls.py delegate a whole URL prefix to an app's own urls.py, keeping routing organized per feature area as the project grows.

Easy
What does include("orders.urls") do in the project-level urls.py?

chapters.title: Defining a model
chapters.lead: A modelA Python class defining the shape of one kind of data, mapped to a database table.press Enter or click to read more
chapters.html:
Part III · Chapter 09

Defining a model

A modelA Python class defining the shape of one kind of data, mapped to a database table.
press Enter or click to read more is a Python class where each attribute becomes a database column, the ORM's bridge between your code and a real table.

from django.db import models

class Restaurant(models.Model):
    name = models.CharField(max_length=100)
    city = models.CharField(max_length=50)
    rating = models.FloatField()
    cost_for_two = models.IntegerField()

    def __str__(self):
        return self.name

This should look immediately familiar: it's the exact same restaurants table from SQLingo, just declared as a Python class instead of a CREATE TABLE statement. A migration (Part IV) is what actually turns this class definition into a real table.

Common trip-ups
  • Forgetting __str__. Without it, objects display as an unhelpful "Restaurant object (1)" everywhere, including the admin panel; defining it costs one line and helps constantly.
  • Defining a model but never running a migration for it. The Python class alone doesn't create the table; a migration (chapter 13-14) has to actually apply that change to the database.
9.1

Recap

A model class, subclassing models.Model, defines fields that become database columns. Migrations (covered next in Part IV) turn that definition into a real table.

Medium
Write a Django model called Customer with fields name (text, up to 100 characters), city (text, up to 50 characters), and joined (a date).

chapters.title: Field types and options
chapters.lead: Beyond the field type itself, options control validation and database-level constraints, directly echoing the "constraints" idea from SQLingo.
chapters.html:
Part III · Chapter 10

Field types and options

Beyond the field type itself, options control validation and database-level constraints, directly echoing the "constraints" idea from SQLingo.

class Order(models.Model):
    amount = models.IntegerField()
    order_date = models.DateField(auto_now_add=True)
    rating_given = models.IntegerField(null=True, blank=True)
    notes = models.CharField(max_length=200, default="")
Common field options
null=Trueallows NULL at the database level (like SQLingo's rating_given)
blank=Trueallows the field to be empty in forms/validation (a separate concern from null)
default=...value used automatically if none is provided
Common trip-ups
  • Confusing null and blank. null is about the database column; blank is about form validation. A field can allow one without the other, and text fields conventionally use blank=True rather than null=True, to avoid two different ways of representing "empty."
10.1

Recap

null=True permits NULL at the database level; blank=True permits an empty value in form validation, two related but distinct concerns.

Medium
What is the difference between null=True and blank=True on a Django model field?

chapters.title: Querying with the ORM
chapters.lead: The ORMLets you work with a database using Python objects instead of writing raw SQL.press Enter or click to read more
chapters.html:
Part III · Chapter 11

Querying with the ORM

The ORMLets you work with a database using Python objects instead of writing raw SQL.
press Enter or click to read more translates Python method calls into real SQL, run against a real database, no raw SQL required for most everyday queries.

Restaurant.objects.filter(city="Mumbai", rating__gte=4.5)
Restaurant.objects.get(id=1)          # exactly one, or raises an error
Restaurant.objects.exclude(city="Delhi")
Restaurant.objects.filter(city="Mumbai").order_by("-rating")

A QuerySetA collection of database rows, represented as Python objects, from an ORM query.
press Enter or click to read more is "lazy," .filter() doesn't hit the database immediately; it builds up a query that only actually runs once you use the result (looping over it, converting to a list, and so on). This lets you chain several filters together before Django builds one final, efficient SQL query.

Common trip-ups
  • Using .get() when a query might return zero or multiple rows. .get() raises an error unless exactly one row matches; use .filter() (returning a QuerySet, possibly empty) when that's not guaranteed.
  • Assuming .filter() immediately queries the database. QuerySets are lazy, useful to know when reasoning about performance.
11.1

Recap

.filter() returns a (possibly empty) lazy QuerySet; .get() returns exactly one object or raises an error. Field lookups like __gte add SQL-level comparisons directly in Python.

Easy
Why might Restaurant.objects.get(id=1) raise an error where .filter(id=1) would not, for the same data?
Medium
Write an ORM query returning all restaurants in "Pune" with a rating of at least 4.0, ordered by rating, highest first.

chapters.title: Relationships: ForeignKey
chapters.lead: A ForeignKey is Django's model-level version of exactly the SQL foreign key relationship SQLingo teaches: one row pointing at another table's row.
chapters.html:
Part III · Chapter 12

Relationships: ForeignKey

A ForeignKey is Django's model-level version of exactly the SQL foreign key relationship SQLingo teaches: one row pointing at another table's row.

class Order(models.Model):
    customer = models.ForeignKey(Customer, on_delete=models.CASCADE)
    restaurant = models.ForeignKey(Restaurant, on_delete=models.CASCADE)
    amount = models.IntegerField()

# following the relationship in Python:
order.customer.name
customer.order_set.all()   # every order this customer has placed

on_delete decides what happens to an Order if its Customer is deleted; CASCADE deletes the order too, matching the referential integrity concerns from SQLingo's own foreign key chapter.

Common trip-ups
  • Forgetting on_delete entirely. Django requires it explicitly, precisely because silently picking a default behaviour for something this consequential would be risky.
  • Choosing CASCADE without thinking it through. Deleting a customer and silently deleting all their historical orders may not actually be the right business behaviour; SET_NULL or PROTECT are sometimes more appropriate.
12.1

Recap

ForeignKey models a real relationship between tables, requiring an explicit on_delete behaviour, and lets you traverse the relationship in both directions directly in Python.

Medium
What does on_delete=models.CASCADE mean on a ForeignKey?

chapters.title: What migrations are, and why
chapters.lead: A migrationA recorded, versioned change to a database's schema.press Enter or click to read more
chapters.html:
Part IV · Chapter 13

What migrations are, and why

A migrationA recorded, versioned change to a database's schema.
press Enter or click to read more is Django's answer to a simple problem: your models describe the database you want, but something has to actually make the real database match.

Migration files are checked into version control alongside your code, so every environment, your machine, a teammate's, staging, production, applies the exact same sequence of schema changes in the exact same order, keeping every database's actual structure in sync with what the code expects.

Common trip-ups
  • Changing a model and forgetting to generate/apply a migration. The Python class and the real database silently disagree until a migration catches them up, which can cause confusing runtime errors.
  • Not committing migration files to version control. Without them, nobody else (or no other environment) can reproduce the same schema changes.
13.1

Recap

Migrations are generated files describing a schema change, applied in order to keep every environment's real database structure in sync with the models in code.

Easy
Why are migration files checked into version control alongside the rest of the code?

chapters.title: makemigrations and migrate
chapters.lead: Two separate commands, each with a distinct job, easy to mix up as a beginner.
chapters.html:
Part IV · Chapter 14

makemigrations and migrate

Two separate commands, each with a distinct job, easy to mix up as a beginner.

$ python manage.py makemigrations
Migrations for 'orders':
  orders/migrations/0004_order_notes.py
    - Add field notes to order

$ python manage.py migrate
Applying orders.0004_order_notes... OK
Two distinct steps
makemigrationslooks at your model changes and generates a migration file describing them (doesn't touch the database yet)
migrateactually applies pending migration files to the real database
Common trip-ups
  • Running makemigrations and assuming the database already changed. It only generates the file; migrate is the step that actually applies it.
  • Running migrate without makemigrations first, after a model change. There's no migration file yet describing the new change, so nothing new gets applied.
14.1

Recap

makemigrations generates a migration file from model changes; migrate applies pending migration files to the real database. Neither replaces the other.

Easy
After running makemigrations, has the real database schema already changed?

chapters.title: Changing a model safely
chapters.lead: Some model changes are simple for Django to figure out automatically; others need a decision only you can make.
chapters.html:
Part IV · Chapter 15

Changing a model safely

Some model changes are simple for Django to figure out automatically; others need a decision only you can make.

Adding a brand-new field with no default value is exactly this kind of case: existing rows already have no value for it, so Django needs to know what to fill in for them. Running makemigrations for such a change interactively asks you to either supply a one-time default, or provide a callable/value in the field itself.

You are trying to add a non-nullable field 'notes' to order
without a default; we can't do that (the database needs
something to populate existing rows).
Please select a fix:
 1) Provide a one-off default now
 2) Quit and add a default in models.py
Common trip-ups
  • Adding a required field with no default to a table that already has real data. This is exactly the scenario above, Django cannot invent a sensible value for existing rows on its own.
  • Renaming a field and expecting Django to know that's what happened. Without extra care, Django may interpret it as removing the old field and adding an unrelated new one, potentially losing data; a genuine rename needs to be handled explicitly.
15.1

Recap

Adding a required field to a table with existing data needs a default, since existing rows need some real value for it. Renaming fields needs explicit care to avoid Django treating it as an unrelated remove-and-add.

Hard
You need to add a required (non-nullable) "phone_number" field to the existing Customer model, which already has real customer rows. What are your options, and what would you actually choose for TastyGo?

chapters.title: Migration gotchas
chapters.lead: A handful of migration mistakes account for the vast majority of real production incidents involving schema changes.
chapters.html:
Part IV · Chapter 16

Migration gotchas

A handful of migration mistakes account for the vast majority of real production incidents involving schema changes.

The big ones
  • Deleting a column that's still in use. Removing a field's migration drops the real database column; if any still-running code expects it, this causes immediate errors.
  • Running migrations without a backup, on a production database with real data. A migration that goes wrong partway through can leave a database in an inconsistent state.
  • Large table migrations locking the table during business hours. Changing a column type on a huge table can take real time and, on some databases, lock it for reads/writes while it runs.

This is exactly the "risk-based testing" and "dev/staging/production" thinking from the QA and Fundamentals courses applied to schema changes specifically: test a migration thoroughly on staging with realistic data volume first, and have a rollback plan before ever running one against production.

16.1

Recap

The riskiest migrations remove or fundamentally change something still in use, or run against a large, live production table without a tested plan and a rollback path.

Medium
Why is testing a migration on staging with realistic data volume important before running it on production?

chapters.title: Function-based views
chapters.lead: A viewA function or class that receives a request and returns a response.press Enter or click to read more
chapters.html:
Part V · Chapter 17

Function-based views

A viewA function or class that receives a request and returns a response.
press Enter or click to read more is where request-handling logic actually lives. The simplest form is just a regular Python function.

from django.shortcuts import render, get_object_or_404

def restaurant_detail(request, restaurant_id):
    restaurant = get_object_or_404(Restaurant, id=restaurant_id)
    return render(request, "restaurant_detail.html", {"restaurant": restaurant})

Every view takes request as its first parameter (plus any URL-captured values, like restaurant_id), and must return an HttpResponse of some kind, render() is a shortcut that renders a template with the given context and wraps it in a response for you.

get_object_or_404 looks a record up and automatically returns a proper 404 response (rather than crashing with an unhandled error) if it doesn't exist, directly echoing the HTTP status code chapter from the QA and Fundamentals courses.

Common trip-ups
  • Forgetting a view must always return a response. A view function that falls through without returning anything raises an error.
  • Using Model.objects.get() directly instead of get_object_or_404. This raises an unhandled 500-style error for a missing record instead of a proper, expected 404.
17.1

Recap

A function-based view takes request (plus URL parameters) and returns a response. get_object_or_404 is the idiomatic way to fetch-or-404 in one step.

Medium
Write a function-based view order_detail(request, order_id) that fetches the order or returns a proper 404, then renders "order_detail.html" with it in context.

chapters.title: Class-based views
chapters.lead: For common patterns, listing objects, showing one object's detail, Django provides ready-made view classes, directly building on this Developer track's Python course's OOP chapters.
chapters.html:
Part V · Chapter 18

Class-based views

For common patterns, listing objects, showing one object's detail, Django provides ready-made view classes, directly building on this Developer track's Python course's OOP chapters.

from django.views.generic import ListView, DetailView

class RestaurantListView(ListView):
    model = Restaurant
    template_name = "restaurant_list.html"
    context_object_name = "restaurants"

class RestaurantDetailView(DetailView):
    model = Restaurant
    template_name = "restaurant_detail.html"

These classes already know how to fetch the right data and render a template, following the exact inheritance pattern taught in the Python course: you're overriding or setting a small number of attributes on a well-tested parent class, rather than writing the whole request-handling flow yourself.

Common trip-ups
  • Reaching for a class-based view when a simple function would be clearer. For genuinely simple, one-off logic, a function-based view can be easier to read; class-based views shine for common, repeatable patterns.
18.1

Recap

Class-based generic views (ListView, DetailView, and others) implement common request-handling patterns for you; you customize by setting attributes or overriding specific methods, not rewriting the whole view.

Medium
What is the main advantage of using ListView instead of writing a function-based view that does the same thing?

chapters.title: Request and response objects
chapters.lead: Every view receives a rich request object and must produce a response object, both worth knowing in some depth.
chapters.html:
Part V · Chapter 19

Request and response objects

Every view receives a rich request object and must produce a response object, both worth knowing in some depth.

Useful bits of the request object
request.method"GET", "POST", etc.
request.GET / request.POSTquery string parameters / submitted form data
request.userthe currently authenticated user (or an anonymous user object)
def search_restaurants(request):
    city = request.GET.get("city", "")
    results = Restaurant.objects.filter(city__icontains=city)
    return render(request, "search.html", {"results": results})

A response can be render() (rendered HTML), JsonResponse (for JSON APIs, foreshadowing FastAPI), or a bare HttpResponse, but a view must always return one of these, exactly as the client-server chapter from the Fundamentals course described: every request gets an answer.

Common trip-ups
  • Reading form data from request.GET on a POST request. Submitted form data lives in request.POST, not request.GET; mixing these up is a common early bug.
19.1

Recap

The request object carries the method, submitted data, and the current user. A view must return a response object of some kind, whether rendered HTML, JSON, or a bare HTTP response.

Easy
Where does submitted form data from a POST request live on the request object?

chapters.title: Handling forms and POST data
chapters.lead: Django's Form classes validate submitted data before you ever touch it, directly applying the QA course's "never trust unvalidated input" principle.
chapters.html:
Part V · Chapter 20

Handling forms and POST data

Django's Form classes validate submitted data before you ever touch it, directly applying the QA course's "never trust unvalidated input" principle.

from django import forms

class OrderForm(forms.Form):
    restaurant_id = forms.IntegerField()
    amount = forms.IntegerField(min_value=1)

def place_order(request):
    if request.method == "POST":
        form = OrderForm(request.POST)
        if form.is_valid():
            # form.cleaned_data holds validated, correctly-typed values
            ...
        else:
            return render(request, "order_form.html", {"form": form})
    return render(request, "order_form.html", {"form": OrderForm()})

form.is_valid() runs all the field-level validation at once (type checks, min/max, required fields), and form.cleaned_data gives back validated, correctly-typed Python values, never raw, unchecked request data.

Common trip-ups
  • Using request.POST data directly without validating it through a Form. This skips type checking and validation entirely, exactly the kind of unvalidated-input risk the QA and Fundamentals security chapters warned about.
  • Forgetting to handle the invalid case. Re-rendering the form with its errors (rather than silently failing) is what lets the user actually fix their mistake.
20.1

Recap

A Form class validates and type-converts submitted data. Always check form.is_valid() before trusting form.cleaned_data, and re-render the form with errors if it's not.

Medium
Why use form.cleaned_data instead of reading request.POST directly?

chapters.title: The template language
chapters.lead: A templateAn HTML file with placeholders filled in with real data before being sent to a browser.press Enter or click to read more
chapters.html:
Part VI · Chapter 21

The template language

A templateAn HTML file with placeholders filled in with real data before being sent to a browser.
press Enter or click to read more is HTML with a small amount of logic mixed in, deliberately less powerful than full Python, to keep presentation logic simple.

<h1>{{ restaurant.name }}</h1>
<p>Rating: {{ restaurant.rating }}</p>

<ul>
{% for order in orders %}
  <li>Order #{{ order.id }}: ₹{{ order.amount }}</li>
{% empty %}
  <li>No orders yet.</li>
{% endfor %}
</ul>
The two syntaxes
{{ variable }}outputs a value
{% tag %}logic: loops, conditionals, template inheritance
Common trip-ups
  • Trying to do heavy logic (complex calculations, database writes) inside a template. Templates are deliberately limited; that logic belongs in the view, keeping presentation and logic cleanly separated, exactly the MTV pattern from chapter 03.
21.1

Recap

{{ }} outputs values; {% %} handles logic like loops and conditionals. Templates are deliberately limited to keep presentation logic simple and separate from view logic.

Easy
What is the correct template syntax for outputting a variable's value?

chapters.title: Template inheritance
chapters.lead: Repeating the same header, navigation, and footer HTML in every single template is exactly the kind of duplication template inheritance exists to avoid.
chapters.html:
Part VI · Chapter 22

Template inheritance

Repeating the same header, navigation, and footer HTML in every single template is exactly the kind of duplication template inheritance exists to avoid.

<!-- base.html -->
<html><body>
  <nav>...TastyGo nav...</nav>
  {% block content %}{% endblock %}
</body></html>

<!-- restaurant_list.html -->
{% extends "base.html" %}
{% block content %}
  <h1>Restaurants</h1>
{% endblock %}

Every page extending base.html automatically gets the shared nav and layout, and only needs to fill in its own {% block content %}, changing the shared layout once updates every page that extends it.

Common trip-ups
  • Copy-pasting the same header/nav HTML into every template. This is exactly the duplication template inheritance solves; a later design change would otherwise need updating in every single file.
22.1

Recap

{% extends %} and {% block %} let child templates inherit shared layout from a base template, overriding only the specific sections that differ.

Easy
What is the main benefit of {% extends "base.html" %} across many templates?

chapters.title: Passing context to templates
chapters.lead: A template only sees exactly what the view explicitly hands it, the "context" dictionary, nothing more.
chapters.html:
Part VI · Chapter 23

Passing context to templates

A template only sees exactly what the view explicitly hands it, the "context" dictionary, nothing more.

def restaurant_detail(request, restaurant_id):
    restaurant = get_object_or_404(Restaurant, id=restaurant_id)
    similar = Restaurant.objects.filter(city=restaurant.city).exclude(id=restaurant.id)[:3]
    return render(request, "restaurant_detail.html", {
        "restaurant": restaurant,
        "similar_restaurants": similar,
    })

The template can use {{ restaurant.name }} and loop over similar_restaurants, but it has no way to reach anything not explicitly passed in this dictionary, reinforcing MTV's separation: the view decides exactly what data is available, the template only decides how to display it.

Common trip-ups
  • Assuming a template can access anything the view has access to. Only what's explicitly included in the context dictionary is visible; forgetting to pass something is a very common source of a template silently rendering blank.
23.1

Recap

The context dictionary is the entire, explicit interface between a view and its template; nothing else the view has access to is automatically visible to it.

Easy
If a view forgets to include "similar_restaurants" in the context dictionary, but the template references {{ similar_restaurants }}, what happens?

chapters.title: Static files
chapters.lead: CSS, JavaScript, and images are "static," they don't change per-request, and Django (and later, a reverse proxy) treats them differently from dynamically rendered HTML.
chapters.html:
Part VI · Chapter 24

Static files

CSS, JavaScript, and images are "static," they don't change per-request, and Django (and later, a reverse proxy) treats them differently from dynamically rendered HTML.

<!-- in a template -->
{% load static %}
<link rel="stylesheet" href="{% static 'css/tastygo.css' %}">
<img src="{% static 'images/logo.png' %}">

In development, Django can serve these files itself for convenience. In production, this is exactly where the Fundamentals course's "web server vs. application server" chapter applies directly: a real web server like nginx serves static files far more efficiently than Django itself should, while Django (the application server) focuses purely on dynamic logic.

Common trip-ups
  • Relying on Django to serve static files efficiently in production. Django's own static file serving is meant for development convenience only; production setups hand this job to a dedicated web server or CDN.
24.1

Recap

Static files (CSS, JS, images) are referenced via {% static %} in templates. Development serves them directly; production hands this job to a dedicated web server, exactly the division of labour covered in the Fundamentals course.

Medium
Why shouldn't Django itself serve static files in production?

chapters.part: Part VII · Admin, auth, and APIs
chapters.title: The Django admin panel
chapters.lead: The admin panelA ready-made web interface for viewing and editing your app's data.press Enter or click to read more
chapters.html:
Part VII · Chapter 25

The Django admin panel

The admin panelA ready-made web interface for viewing and editing your app's data.
press Enter or click to read more is one of Django's most famous features: a working interface to your data, generated automatically, with almost no extra code.

from django.contrib import admin
from .models import Restaurant

admin.site.register(Restaurant)
NameCityRating
Domino'sMumbai4.8
KFCBangalore4.3

Three lines of code, and TastyGo staff already have a working, permission-aware interface for browsing and editing restaurant data, no custom HTML, forms, or views written by hand. This is enormously useful for internal tools and content management, though it's rarely the right interface for real customers.

Common trip-ups
  • Exposing the admin panel to customers as their main interface. It's built for trusted staff managing data, not designed as a customer-facing product experience.
  • Leaving the default admin URL and weak credentials in production. The admin panel is a high-value target; strong credentials and access restrictions matter, covered further in chapter 26.
25.1

Recap

admin.site.register(Model) generates a full working admin interface for that model automatically, ideal for internal/staff data management, not intended as a customer-facing product surface.

Easy
What does admin.site.register(Restaurant) provide?

chapters.part: Part VII · Admin, auth, and APIs
chapters.title: Authentication and permissions
chapters.lead: Django ships with a complete user authentication system built in, directly implementing the authentication-vs-authorization distinction from the Fundamentals course.
chapters.html:
Part VII · Chapter 26

Authentication and permissions

Django ships with a complete user authentication system built in, directly implementing the authentication-vs-authorization distinction from the Fundamentals course.

from django.contrib.auth.decorators import login_required

@login_required
def my_orders(request):
    orders = Order.objects.filter(customer=request.user.customer)
    return render(request, "my_orders.html", {"orders": orders})
Two distinct checks, again
@login_requiredauthentication: is anyone actually logged in?
filtering by request.userauthorization: showing only this user's own orders, not everyone's

@login_required alone only checks that someone is logged in; it says nothing about which specific orders they should be allowed to see. That second check, filtering by request.user, is what actually prevents one customer from viewing another's private order history, exactly the QA course's security testing scenario.

Common trip-ups
  • Assuming @login_required alone is enough security. It only proves someone is logged in, not that they're allowed to see this specific data; a view still needs its own authorization logic.
26.1

Recap

@login_required handles authentication (is someone logged in). Filtering data by request.user (or explicit permission checks) handles authorization (are they allowed to see this specific data), and both are needed together.

Hard
A view uses @login_required but queries Order.objects.all() instead of filtering by the logged-in customer. What security bug does this create, and how would you fix it?

chapters.part: Part VII · Admin, auth, and APIs
chapters.title: Django REST Framework, briefly
chapters.lead: Django itself renders HTML templates by default. When TastyGo needs a real JSON API instead, for a mobile app, or another service, Django REST Framework (DRF) is the standard way to build it.
chapters.html:
Part VII · Chapter 27

Django REST Framework, briefly

Django itself renders HTML templates by default. When TastyGo needs a real JSON API instead, for a mobile app, or another service, Django REST Framework (DRF) is the standard way to build it.

from rest_framework import serializers, viewsets

class RestaurantSerializer(serializers.ModelSerializer):
    class Meta:
        model = Restaurant
        fields = ["id", "name", "city", "rating"]

class RestaurantViewSet(viewsets.ModelViewSet):
    queryset = Restaurant.objects.all()
    serializer_class = RestaurantSerializer

A serializer converts model instances to and from JSON, directly building on the Python course's json.dumps/loads chapter, and a ViewSet bundles up the typical list/create/retrieve/update/delete operations for a model into ready-made API endpoints.

This is deliberately a brief introduction: the FastAPI course, next in this Developer track, goes deep on building APIs, using a framework built for that job specifically from the ground up, rather than as an addition to a full web framework.

Common trip-ups
  • Building a JSON API by hand-writing JsonResponse everywhere. This works for something tiny, but DRF's serializers and ViewSets remove a large amount of repetitive boilerplate for anything more substantial.
27.1

Recap

Django REST Framework adds serializers (model <-> JSON conversion) and ViewSets (ready-made CRUD API endpoints) on top of Django, for when you need a real JSON API rather than rendered HTML.

Easy
What is the main job of a DRF serializer?

chapters.title: Settings for production
chapters.lead: A handful of settings.py changes separate a safe, real deployment from a development setup accidentally left exposed to the internet.
chapters.html:
Part VIII · Chapter 28

Settings for production

A handful of settings.py changes separate a safe, real deployment from a development setup accidentally left exposed to the internet.

# development
DEBUG = True
ALLOWED_HOSTS = []
SECRET_KEY = "hardcoded-for-convenience"

# production
DEBUG = False
ALLOWED_HOSTS = ["tastygo.com"]
SECRET_KEY = os.environ["DJANGO_SECRET_KEY"]  # from environment, never committed
Why each one matters
DEBUG = Falsestops detailed error pages (with internal code/settings) from ever reaching real users
ALLOWED_HOSTSrejects requests claiming to be for a domain this server doesn't actually serve
SECRET_KEY from environmentkeeps a sensitive value out of version control entirely

This connects directly to the Fundamentals course's dev/staging/production chapter: the same codebase behaves differently depending on environment-specific settings like these, deliberately kept separate from the code itself.

Common trip-ups
  • Deploying with DEBUG = True. This can leak internal stack traces, settings, and file paths directly to any user who triggers an error.
  • Committing SECRET_KEY (or any real credential) into version control. Once committed, it should be considered compromised, even if later removed, since it may remain in the repository's history.
28.1

Recap

Production settings must set DEBUG = False, configure ALLOWED_HOSTS, and load secrets from the environment rather than hardcoding or committing them.

Easy
Why is DEBUG = True dangerous in a production deployment?

chapters.title: Where to go next: FastAPI, DevOps, Capstone
chapters.navTitle: Where to go next: FastAPI, DevOps, Capstone
chapters.lead: You can now build a real, working Django application: models backed by a real database, views handling requests, templates rendering HTML, an admin panel, and authentication.
chapters.html:
Part VIII · Chapter 29

Where to go next: FastAPI, DevOps, Capstone

You can now build a real, working Django application: models backed by a real database, views handling requests, templates rendering HTML, an admin panel, and authentication.

What's ahead
FastAPIa different, leaner approach specifically for building fast, well-documented APIs, worth learning even if Django ends up your main framework
DevOpsactually deploying a Django app: WSGI/ASGI servers, a reverse proxy in front, containers, CI/CD
Capstone Projectbuilding, containerizing, and deploying a real slice of TastyGo, likely using exactly what this course just taught

Django is a strong, complete choice for a full web application with server-rendered pages and an admin panel. FastAPI, taught next, optimizes for a different shape of problem: a lean, fast, well-documented JSON API, often serving a separate frontend or mobile app rather than rendering HTML itself.

29.1

Recap

This course built real fluency in Django's MTV pattern, ORM, migrations, views, templates, admin, and auth. FastAPI next explores a different, API-first approach, and DevOps covers actually shipping either one.

Easy
Which is a more accurate description of when Django tends to be the stronger choice compared to FastAPI?

extras.title: Django interview questions
extras.lead: A deep, topic-by-topic bank of the Django questions asked in real interviews, grouped by area, with concise answers and the reasoning interviewers listen for. Click any question to expand it.
extras.html:
Interview prep

Django interview questions

A deep, topic-by-topic bank of the Django questions asked in real interviews, grouped by area, with concise answers and the reasoning interviewers listen for. Click any question to expand it.

Fundamentals

BeginnerWhat is Django, and what does "batteries included" mean?

A high-level Python web framework that ships with an ORM, admin, auth, forms, templating, migrations and security defaults out of the box — so you build features instead of plumbing.

BeginnerWhat is the MTV pattern (and how does it map to MVC)?

Django is Model-Template-View: Model = data, Template = presentation, View = logic that ties them together. It maps to MVC where Django "View" is the controller and the "Template" is the view.

IntermediateWalk through the request-response lifecycle.

The URLconf matches the path to a view, request middleware runs, the view executes (querying models via the ORM), a template renders the response, response middleware runs, and Django returns it.

Request→URLconf→Middleware→View→Model / ORM→Template→Response
BeginnerProject vs app in Django?

A project is the whole site and its settings; an app is a reusable, self-contained module of functionality (e.g. orders). A project contains many apps.

Models & the ORM

BeginnerWhat is the Django ORM?

An object-relational mapper: you define models as Python classes and query with Python instead of raw SQL. Django generates the SQL and returns model instances.

Beginnermakemigrations vs migrate?

makemigrations turns model changes into migration files (a versioned change plan); migrate applies those files to the database. One authors the change, the other executes it.

IntermediateQuerySets are lazy — what does that mean?

Building a QuerySet does not hit the database; the query runs only when you evaluate it (iterate, slice, list(), etc.). This lets you chain filters efficiently, but watch for accidental repeated evaluation.

Advancedselect_related vs prefetch_related, and the N+1 problem?

The N+1 problem is one query for a list plus one extra per row for a relation. select_related fixes it for FK/one-to-one via a SQL JOIN; prefetch_related fixes it for many-to-many/reverse FK with a second query joined in Python.

IntermediateExplain the model relationship fields.

ForeignKey = many-to-one, ManyToManyField = many-to-many (via a join table), OneToOneField = one-to-one. Each defines how rows link across tables.

Intermediatenull vs blank on a model field?

null is database-level (is NULL allowed in the column); blank is validation-level (may the form field be empty). They are independent — a common gotcha.

Views, URLs & middleware

IntermediateFunction-based vs class-based views?

FBVs are simple and explicit — good for one-off logic. CBVs use inheritance and mixins to reuse common patterns (list, detail, create) with less code, at the cost of a steeper learning curve.

BeginnerHow does URL routing work?

urls.py maps URL patterns to views; Django tries them top to bottom and calls the first match, passing captured path parameters to the view.

IntermediateWhat is middleware?

Hooks that wrap every request/response — used for auth, sessions, CSRF, security headers, logging. Each middleware can act before the view (on the request) and after (on the response), in order.

Templates, forms & security

BeginnerWhat does the template system do?

It renders HTML from templates with context data, using tags/filters and auto-escaping output to prevent XSS. Logic is intentionally limited to keep presentation and business logic separate.

IntermediateForms vs ModelForms?

A Form defines fields and validation manually; a ModelForm builds fields automatically from a model and can save directly to it. Both centralise validation and rendering.

IntermediateWhat is CSRF protection and how does Django handle it?

Cross-Site Request Forgery tricks a logged-in user into submitting an unwanted request. Django issues a per-session CSRF token that must accompany unsafe requests (POST/PUT/DELETE); the middleware rejects mismatches.

AdvancedWhat security protections does Django provide by default?

Auto-escaped templates (XSS), CSRF tokens, ORM-parameterised queries (SQL injection), clickjacking protection (X-Frame-Options), secure password hashing, and settings for HTTPS/HSTS/secure cookies.

Admin, auth & APIs

BeginnerWhat is the Django admin?

An auto-generated, model-driven web interface for CRUD on your data — great for internal/staff tooling with almost no code. You register models and customise list/detail views.

IntermediateAuthentication vs authorization in Django?

Authentication = who you are (the auth system, login, sessions). Authorization = what you may do (permissions, groups, @login_required, object-level checks).

IntermediateWhat is Django REST Framework and a serializer?

DRF is a toolkit for building web APIs on Django. A serializer converts model instances to/from JSON and validates incoming data — the API equivalent of a form.

Advanced

AdvancedWhat are signals, and when should you be cautious with them?

Signals let decoupled code react to events (e.g. post_save). Handy for cross-cutting side effects, but overuse hides control flow and makes debugging harder — prefer explicit calls when the logic belongs together.

AdvancedStatic files vs media files?

Static files are code assets you ship (CSS, JS, images), collected via collectstatic. Media files are user-uploaded content stored at runtime. They are served and secured differently.

AdvancedWSGI vs ASGI?

WSGI is the traditional synchronous Python web-server interface. ASGI is its async successor, enabling async views, WebSockets and long-lived connections. Modern Django supports both.

IntermediateHow do you manage settings across environments?

Keep secrets and environment-specific values out of code — read them from environment variables (dev/staging/prod), split settings modules, and never commit SECRET_KEY or credentials.

AdvancedHow do you improve Django performance?

Kill N+1 queries (select_related/prefetch_related), add DB indexes, cache expensive views/queries, use pagination, defer/only heavy columns, and profile with Django Debug Toolbar.

extras.title: Cheat sheet
extras.lead: Every chapter's core idea on one page.
extras.html:
Quick reference

Cheat sheet

Every chapter's core idea on one page.

Groundwork

00 What is Django, and why a "framework" at all?
Django is a batteries-included Python web framework: routing, database access, templating, admin, and security defaults, all provided.
0b Django's toolkit: manage.py, apps, and project structure
manage.py is the command-line entry point. A project is the whole site; apps are self-contained feature areas within it.
0i Meet the project: building TastyGo's backend in Django
TastyGo's Django project mirrors SQLingo's own tables: customers, restaurants, orders apps.

Part I · Project & app structure

01 Projects vs. apps
Split apps by feature area, self-contained but allowed to reference each other; avoid splitting too finely or dumping everything in one giant app.
02 settings.py and configuration
INSTALLED_APPS = [...]   # which apps are active
DATABASES = {...}        # which real database to connect to
DEBUG = True/False       # never True in production
03 The MTV pattern
Request -> urls.py (router) -> View -> Model (ORM, data) -> Template (HTML) -> Response
04 The dev server, conceptually
python manage.py runserver   # local dev only, never production

Part II · URL routing

05 urls.py and URL patterns
urlpatterns = [path("restaurants/", views.restaurant_list), ...]
# checked top to bottom; first match wins
06 Path converters and dynamic URLs
path("orders//", views.order_detail)
# , ,  capture and convert part of the URL
07 Named URLs and reverse()
path(..., name="order-detail")
reverse("order-detail", args=[42])   # generates the URL by name, not hard-coded text
08 Including an app's URLs
path("orders/", include("orders.urls"))   # delegates a URL prefix to an app's own urls.py

Part III · Models & the ORM

09 Defining a model
class Restaurant(models.Model):
    name = models.CharField(max_length=100)
    rating = models.FloatField()
# a model class = a database table
10 Field types and options
null=True     # DB column can be NULL
blank=True    # form validation allows empty
default=...   # used if no value given
11 Querying with the ORM
Model.objects.filter(...)   # lazy QuerySet, possibly empty
Model.objects.get(...)      # exactly one, or raises an error
12 Relationships: ForeignKey
customer = models.ForeignKey(Customer, on_delete=models.CASCADE)
# on_delete is required: CASCADE, SET_NULL, PROTECT, ...

Part IV · Migrations

13 What migrations are, and why
Model change -> makemigrations (generates file) -> migrate (applies to real DB). Both committed to version control.
14 makemigrations and migrate
python manage.py makemigrations   # generate migration file
python manage.py migrate          # apply it to the database
15 Changing a model safely
Adding a required field to a table with existing data needs a default, or the field must allow null.
16 Migration gotchas
Riskiest migrations: dropping a still-used column, no backup, or locking a large production table. Test on staging with realistic data first.

Part V · Views

17 Function-based views
def view(request, id):
    obj = get_object_or_404(Model, id=id)
    return render(request, "template.html", {"obj": obj})
18 Class-based views
class RestaurantListView(ListView):
    model = Restaurant
# common patterns (list/detail) with no boilerplate
19 Request and response objects
request.method, request.GET, request.POST, request.user
# a view must always return a response (render/JsonResponse/HttpResponse)
20 Handling forms and POST data
form = OrderForm(request.POST)
if form.is_valid():
    form.cleaned_data   # validated, correctly-typed values

Part VI · Templates

21 The template language
{{ variable }}   # output
{% tag %}        # logic: loops, conditionals, inheritance
22 Template inheritance
{% extends "base.html" %}
{% block content %}...{% endblock %}   # shared layout, overridden per-page
23 Passing context to templates
render(request, "t.html", {"key": value})
# template only sees exactly what's in this context dict
24 Static files
{% static 'css/tastygo.css' %}
# dev: Django can serve it. production: a real web server (nginx) should.

Part VII · Admin, auth, and APIs

25 The Django admin panel
admin.site.register(Restaurant)
# instant staff interface, generated from the model, not for customers
26 Authentication and permissions
@login_required          # authentication: is anyone logged in?
filter(customer=request.user...)   # authorization: is it THEIR data?
27 Django REST Framework, briefly
ModelSerializer   # model <-> JSON
ModelViewSet      # ready-made CRUD API endpoints (Django REST Framework)

Part VIII · Shipping it

28 Settings for production
DEBUG = False
ALLOWED_HOSTS = ["tastygo.com"]
SECRET_KEY = os.environ["DJANGO_SECRET_KEY"]
29 Where to go next: FastAPI, DevOps, Capstone
Django = full app, server-rendered, admin included. FastAPI = lean, API-first. DevOps = deploying either for real.
